question: A patient with a prior ascending aortic graft and median sternotomy underwent CTA as part of evaluation for new hemoptysis. Based on the imaging shown, which additional symptom or clinical sign would be most likely?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the axial contrast CT image of the upper chest. I see a contrast-filled sac-like outpouching at the anterior aspect of the ascending aortic graft that projects up against the posterior table of the sternum. Immediately anterior to that outpouching there is discontinuity of a sternal wire and a focal area where the wire abuts the vascular sac, consistent with a pseudoaneurysm adjacent to the sternum.

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the clinical question about additional likely signs or symptoms in this patient with a prior ascending aortic graft and median sternotomy who has new hemoptysis. A pseudoaneurysm of the ascending aorta that bulges anteriorly and contacts the sternum is anatomically poised to produce a palpable or visible anterior chest wall pulsation because the vascular sac lies directly beneath the sternum and anterior chest wall.

Then, I consider the answer choices relative to the image. Findings suggesting infection (persistent fever/chills or purulent cough) are not evident on this CT slice and are not specifically indicated by the contained vascular outpouching. Lower-extremity swelling and acute focal neurologic deficit are systemic or embolic manifestations that are not directly supported by the localized anterior mediastinal/pseudoaneurysm appearance. In contrast, a pulsatile anterior chest wall mass is the most direct clinical correlate of an anteriorly projecting ascending aortic pseudoaneurysm abutting the sternum.

Therefore, the imaging most strongly supports the presence of a pulsatile anterior chest wall mass.
caption: A The CTA performed six months before presenting the hemoptysis. Note the proximity of the sternal wire and the graft and the absence of any space between the sternum and the aortic graft. B and C The recent CTA (axial and lateral views): the thin arrow shows the fractured wire, and the thick arrow shows the pseudoaneurysm. Note the large space filled with a hematoma between the graft and the posterior border of the sternum
sub_caption: B and C The recent CTA (axial and lateral views): the thin arrow shows the fractured wire, and the thick arrow shows the pseudoaneurysm. Note the large space filled with a hematoma between the graft and the posterior border of the sternum.
image_context: Finding out the cause of hemoptysis, he was admitted and a comprehensive evaluation including computed tomography angiography (CTA), bronchoscopy, and transesophageal echocardiography set out. The CTA showed a new collection, most probably a pseudoaneurysm sized 4 × 6 × 9 cm around the ascending aorta extending to the posterior border of the manubrium at the maximum diameter (Fig. <xref rid="13019_2021_1737_Fig1_HTML" ref-type="fig">1</xref>B, C). Therefore, we focused on the ascending aorta as the source of bleeding into the mediastinum, which could have caused an aortobronchial fistula and ultimately the hemoptysis. The source images of a CTA belonging to 6 months earlier were reviewed. We noticed a sternal wire applied with the figure-of-eight technique in the first surgery in close contact with the Dacron graft, and it seemed to have penetrated the graft (Fig. B, C). Therefore, we focused on the ascending aorta as the source of bleeding into the mediastinum, which could have caused an aortobronchial fistula and ultimately the hemoptysis. The source images of a CTA belonging to 6 months earlier were reviewed. We noticed a sternal wire applied with the figure-of-eight technique in the first surgery in close contact with the Dacron graft, and it seemed to have penetrated the graft (Fig. <xref rid="13019_2021_1737_Fig1_HTML" ref-type="fig">1</xref>A). We also spotted a fracture line on the wire without any displacement. Viewing a large space between the wire and the graft occupied by a pseudoaneurysm in the latest CTA corroborated the assumption that the graft erosion was established by the wire.A). We also spotted a fracture line on the wire without any displacement. Note the proximity of the sternal wire and the graft and the absence of any space between the sternum and the aortic graft. B and C The recent CTA (axial and lateral views): the thin arrow shows the fractured wire, and the thick arrow shows the pseudoaneurysm. Note the large space filled with a hematoma between the graft and the posterior border of the sternum
relevant_image_context: Finding out the cause of hemoptysis, the patient underwent a comprehensive evaluation including computed tomography angiography (CTA), bronchoscopy, and transesophageal echocardiography. The CTA showed a new collection around the ascending aorta extending to the posterior border of the manubrium at the maximum diameter. Therefore, the ascending aorta was considered the source of bleeding into the mediastinum, with the possibility of an aortobronchial fistula and hemoptysis. The source images of a CTA from six months earlier were reviewed. We noted a sternal wire applied with the figure-of-eight technique in the initial surgery in close contact with the Dacron graft, and it appeared to have penetrated the graft. We also observed a fracture line on the wire without displacement. Viewing a large space between the wire and the graft occupied by a pseudoaneurysm in the latest CTA supported the conclusion that graft erosion was caused by the wire.
